feat(memories): safe batch delete to fix 429 on web bulk selection (#10364) ## Problem When selecting many memories in the web app and clicking **Delete**, the frontend issued one `DELETE /v3/memories/{id}` per selected memory concurrently via `Promise.all()`. With a large selection (50+) this immediately blows through the per-UID rate limiter / Cloud Armor and the requests come back **429** — so a bulk delete of a page of memories fails for many users. ## Root cause No batch path existed, so the only way to delete *N* memories was *N* independent HTTP requests. Each is a separately rate-limited destructive call, and fanning them out concurrently trips the rate limit before the work completes. ## What this PR does Adds a single **`DELETE /v3/memories/batch`** endpoint (≤ `MEMORIES_BATCH_MAX` = 100 ids/request) and switches the web bulk-delete flow to use it — one HTTP request per page of selections instead of *N*. ### Authorization guard (the important part) This rebuilds the approach attempted in #7006, which was blocked because its batch endpoint validated the selection with `get_memories_by_ids()` and only checked existence — letting a caller include `is_locked` (paid-plan) memories in `memory_ids` and delete them through the batch route, even though the single-delete route rejects that with **402**. This PR preserves the **exact** authorization/business semantics of `DELETE /v3/memories/{memory_id}`: - Every requested id is validated with the **same** rules as the single-delete path: - **missing / not-owned → 404** (`get_memories_by_ids` is scoped to the caller's uid collection, so another user's id simply isn't returned) - **locked (paid-plan) memory → 402** (`is_locked`), mirroring `_validate_memory()` / `fetch_memory_dict()` - Validation is **all-or-nothing**: if any id fails, **nothing is deleted**, so the batch route can never act as a bypass for the single-delete guardrails. This now holds for **both** cohorts: - **Legacy** validates the whole selection via one batched `get_all` fetch, then a single batched Firestore write. - **Canonical** validates and tombstones the full uid-scoped active selection in one Firestore transaction. The transaction reads every item before queuing any evidence/item/outbox writes; concurrent changes retry the whole transaction, and missing/non-active items abort before commit. Derived vector, keyword, conflict, and KG cleanup runs best-effort only after the authoritative commit. - The legacy cohort validates via a single batched `get_all` fetch instead of *N* serial reads (the efficiency win #7006 was after), then one batched Firestore write. The canonical cohort mirrors the single-delete canonical path exactly (`MemoryService.delete`, 404 on not-found). - Vector cleanup reuses the existing chunked `delete_memory_vectors_batch` helper and stays best-effort (logged, never fails the request) — identical to single-delete. - Input ids are deduplicated (order-preserving) before any DB call. - Dedicated `memories:delete_batch` rate policy (10 req/hour) — tighter than `memories:delete` (60/hour) since each request can remove up to 100 memories. ### Partial-failure contract (web) For selections over the per-request bound, `removeMemories()` splits ids into chunks of 100 and sends sequential batch requests. **Each successful chunk updates the UI immediately**, so a later chunk failure can never leave already-deleted items visible. On full success the selection clears; on partial failure `removeMemories` returns the set of **confirmed-deleted** ids (`deletedIds`), and `executeBulkDelete` prunes those from `selectedIds` so a retry only re-sends the failed / un-attempted ids — it never re-sends ids the server already removed (which the all-or-nothing endpoint would 404 on). ### Proxy The Next.js dev proxy (`/api/proxy/[...path]`) previously dropped the request body and `Content-Type` for `DELETE`, so a `DELETE` with a JSON body never reached the backend in local dev. It now forwards the body and sets `Content-Type: application/json` for `DELETE` like it already did for `POST`/`PATCH`. *(This commit is cherry-picked from #7006 — its only piece that was correct and applied cleanly.)* ## Changes | Area | File | Change | |------|------|--------| | backend | `routers/memories.py` | `DELETE /v3/memories/batch` + `BatchDeleteMemoriesRequest`; registered **before** `/v3/memories/{memory_id}` so `batch` isn't captured as a path param. Canonical cohort now validates and tombstones the full selection in one Firestore transaction, providing true all-or-nothing mutation. | | backend | `database/memories.py` | `delete_memories_batch()` — batched Firestore write (mirrors `delete_all_memories` chunking) | | backend | `utils/rate_limit_config.py` | `memories:delete_batch` policy (10/hour) | | backend tests | `tests/unit/test_memories_batch_delete.py` | new — auth parity, dedup, happy path, **canonical all-or-nothing regression** (earlier valid id not tombstoned when a later id is missing), route ordering, model + policy | | backend tests | `test_memories_create.py`, `test_rate_limiting.py` | updated rate-limit wiring count (10→11) + per-endpoint wiring assertions | | contract | `docs/api-reference/app-client-openapi.json` | regenerated via `export_openapi.py --surface app-client --write` to include the new DELETE route + `BatchDeleteMemoriesRequest` | | contract | `*/omiApi.generated.ts` (web/app, web/admin, desktop/windows, personas-open-source) | regenerated via `generate_ts_openapi_types.py` from the updated spec | | web | `lib/api.ts` | `deleteMemoriesBatch(ids)` | | web | `hooks/useMemories.ts` | `removeMemories(ids)` returns `{ success, deletedIds }`; fix(sync): stop routing all offline recordings through the bounded backfill lane (#10400) Offline recordings uploaded successfully and then sat unprocessed for many hours. The queue backing the historical-recovery lane had built up ~14.7k tasks, some scheduled almost a day after they were created, while the workers behind it were idle. ## What was happening `classify_sync_lane` marks an upload `fresh` only when the server can corroborate its capture time against a conversation it already knows about. An offline recording has no such conversation — the server was not in the loop when it was captured — so **every offline upload classifies as `backfill`**, regardless of age. Production shows recordings thirty seconds old admitted as `lane=backfill trust=legacy reason=unbound_capture_time`. That sent the entire offline workload to a lane provisioned for occasional historical recovery: a worker capped at four instances at one request each, behind a queue offering four dispatches at a time. The failure was not throughput, it was compounding retry delay: 1. dispatch exceeds what four instances can admit 2. Cloud Run rejects the surplus with "no available instance" 3. Cloud Tasks retries each rejection with exponential backoff, up to an hour 4. tasks end up scheduled hours out, so few are eligible at any moment 5. the queue looks enormous while the workers have nothing to run Raising the worker to thirty barely moved throughput, because the backlog was waiting on backoff rather than capacity. Bounding the backoff took completions from roughly eight per minute to fifty, with rejections falling to zero. ## Changes **Route every sync job to the main queue.** The main queue has no dispatch cap, which is what offline sync ran on before the lane split. The lane label stays on the payload for metering and reporting; it no longer selects the queue. **Size the lane so dispatch cannot outrun the worker.** Dispatch concurrency now equals the worker's instance ceiling — offering more than the worker admits is what produced the rejections. A warm instance keeps a scale-from-zero poke from being rejected outright, and retry backoff is bounded so one transient rejection cannot strand a recording for hours. The deploy action previously hardcoded the old four-and-four sizing on every run, so any manual tuning was silently reverted by the next deploy. ## Tests `test_backfill_lane_still_uses_the_main_queue` asserts a payload carrying the backfill lane enqueues to the main queue and handler, with the label preserved. The lifecycle contract test asserts the matched sizing and the bounded backoff. `tests/unit/test_sync_two_lane.py` and `tests/unit/test_sync_v2.py` — 199 passed. Nine failures in `test_sync_cloud_tasks.py` reproduce identically on an unmodified checkout; they are a `fakeredis` artifact in this environment and are unrelated to this change. Failure-Class: none

fix(agent): back off the journal outbox pump so a poison row can't hot-loop the event loop

The journal outbox pump ran on a fixed 1s setInterval and swallowed any throw
from its drains. The prior commit fixed the one known poison (canonical-hash
mismatch), but the pump is still one require* throw away from the same wedge: a
missing journal turn/state row makes drainBackendTurnOutbox throw, and a fixed
interval re-throws it every second forever, surviving restarts (the exact shape
that froze chat mid-stream and spammed stderr at 1Hz).

Close the failure class at the pump instead of chasing each poison: convert the
timer to a self-rescheduling setTimeout with exponential backoff (base 1s,
capped ~1/min) driven by a consecutive-failure streak, and have pumpJournalOutbox
report whether it ran cleanly. A single clean pump snaps the cadence back to
base. Any future poison now throttles to one attempt (and one log line) per
minute rather than pinning the event loop — and it logs a one-line recovery
notice when it clears. Also wires the onQuarantine log from the prior fix.

The agent has no fallback-telemetry primitive (that contract is Swift/Rust), so
visibility here is the bounded backoff plus the recovery log line; the Swift side
still records the fallback when a turn fails to sync.

Failure-Class: FC-outbox-pump-poison-wedge | none

Verification: new hermetic test tests/journal-pump-backoff.test.ts covers the
backoff curve (base/doubling/cap/reset/non-finite). Full node suite 591/591 pass
(was 586 before these two fixes). tsc introduces no new errors in the touched
files.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix(agent): quarantine canonical-hash-mismatched outbox rows instead of wedging the pump

drainBackendTurnOutbox threw when a backend_turn_outbox row's stored
payload_hash diverged from the canonical journal turn. The throw aborted the
whole batch transaction, so the 1s journal outbox pump re-selected the same
poisoned row every tick and never drained any turn. Because the row is durable,
the loop began one second after bridge startup and survived restarts, spamming
"Backend turn outbox revision does not match the canonical journal turn" at 1Hz
forever and leaving in-flight chat turns stuck mid-stream with a spinning loader
(observed in the omi-auth-stall-qa bundle).

Fail closed on the divergence as before, but per-row: park the mismatched row
(status='failed', last_error_code='canonical_hash_mismatch', lease cleared) and
continue draining. Healthy turns still deliver, the poisoned row is never
re-selected, and a later updateJournalTurn re-stamps and re-arms it. An optional
onQuarantine callback lets the pump log the event once instead of at 1Hz.

Failure-Class: FC-outbox-pump-poison-wedge | new

Verification: added a regression test in conversation-journal.test.ts (no throw;
healthy turn delivers; row parks with the diagnostic code; re-drain does not
re-select it; a later mutation re-arms delivery). Full node suite
conversation-journal.test.ts 43/43 pass. tsc introduces no new errors in the
touched files (pre-existing worktree node-types resolution noise only).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix(desktop): grow home ask bar to fit multi-line messages (#10411)

## Problem

On the desktop home screen, the **"Ask omi anything"** chat bar used a
single-line `TextField` pinned to a fixed `.frame(height: 58)` pill.
When the typed text got longer than the bar's width, the bar did not
grow — the text just scrolled horizontally out of view, so you couldn't
see what you'd typed.

## Fix

`HomeAskBar` now uses a vertical, auto-growing `TextField`:

- `axis: .vertical` + `lineLimit(1...6)` — the bar grows as text wraps,
up to six lines, then scrolls internally.
- Removed the fixed `.frame(height: 58)`; the pill now uses `minHeight:
58` (unchanged single-line look) and grows with content.
- Row is bottom-aligned so the paperclip / send / connect controls sit
with the last line as the bar expands — the standard chat-input pattern.
- `Return` submits (matching the main chat input); `Shift+Return`
inserts a newline.
- Corner radius stays `29` (a capsule when single-line, a clean rounded
rectangle when taller).

No other layout depends on the old fixed 58pt height (the bar is only
ever width-constrained at its call sites).

## Verification

- `xcrun swift build -c debug --package-path Desktop` — **clean** (from
a fresh worktree).
- `xcrun swift build -c release --triple arm64-apple-macosx` — clean.
- Built and ran a signed named bundle (`omi-askbar`); exercised the bar:
long text now wraps and grows the pill instead of scrolling off-screen,
Enter sends, Shift+Enter adds a newline.
- Local CI-parity checks pass: `check-e2e-flow-coverage.py --strict`
(DashboardPage covered), `check-desktop-changelog.py`,
`desktop-changelog.py validate`.

Added
`desktop/macos/changelog/unreleased/20260723-home-ask-bar-grows.json`.

fix(desktop): remove black gap above Home chat panel (#10410)

## Problem

On the Home tab, the inline chat panel had a large black gap between the
shell top bar and the first chat message — the chat didn't reach up
"almost to the top bar."

## Cause

`homeStage` applied a fixed `homeStageTopPadding = 74` to **every**
mode. That clearance is right for the optically-centered hub, but the
chat/connect **panel** modes fill the height and read as the resting
Home surface. Since the Capture/Listening header moved into the shell's
constant `DesktopTopBar` (and page content sits directly below it in a
`VStack`, no underlap), that 74pt simply stranded a tall black band
under the top bar.

## Fix

Add `homePanelStageTopPadding = 16` and apply it in panel (chat/connect)
modes, keeping the hub's 74pt:

```swift
.padding(.top, homeMode == .hub ? Self.homeStageTopPadding : Self.homePanelStageTopPadding)
```

The chat now sits just below the top bar. Hub layout is unchanged.

## Verification

- Clean `swift build` of the full desktop app; `swift-format` clean.
- Built + signed + ran a named test bundle (`omi-topgap`) signed-in
against the prod backend, Home tab in chat mode — the black band is gone
and messages start right under the top bar (verified via screenshot).
- Scope is one mode-gated padding value; hub unchanged, fully
reversible.


Failure-Class: none

fix: recycle poisoned Parakeet GPU workers (#10413)

## What changed and why

Parakeet could keep a replica Ready after a fatal CUDA stream-capture
error, so one poisoned process served only HTTP 500s indefinitely. This
disables CUDA graph decoding when batch and streaming inference share
the GPU, makes fatal accelerator errors poison the authoritative GPU
worker state, and lets health probes recycle the replica.

It also adds per-pod black-hole and fatal-CUDA alerts and fixes the
dashboard to use Parakeet's `success`/`error` status labels.

## Product invariants affected

none

## How it was verified

- Recycled the original poisoned production pod and observed its
replacement become Ready and serve successful transcriptions with no
CUDA/HTTP 500 errors.
- The live Prometheus form of the new black-hole query then detected the
same failure recurring on that replacement. Production logs confirmed
the exact `cudaErrorStreamCaptureUnsupported` failure.
- Completed a rolling Parakeet-only configuration change to
`PARAKEET_CUDA_GRAPHS=false` while the healthy sibling continued
serving. Both replacement replicas became Ready with healthy `/health`
responses and served 211 real transcriptions immediately after rollout
(209 + 2), with zero HTTP 500s and zero fatal-CUDA signatures.
- `backend/.venv/bin/python -m pytest -q
backend/tests/unit/test_parakeet_gpu_worker.py
backend/tests/unit/test_parakeet_endpoints.py
backend/tests/unit/test_parakeet_stream_session.py
backend/tests/unit/test_parakeet_helm_contract.py
backend/tests/unit/test_monitoring_alert_rule_contract.py` — 149 passed.
- `make runtime-image-source-closure` — all 10 registered runtime-image
contracts passed.
- `PYTHON=.venv/bin/python bash test-preflight.sh` — 18 checks passed, 0
failed.
- `OMI_PR_BODY_FILE=/tmp/parakeet-pr-body.md make preflight` — all 20
diff-scoped checks passed; the bounded pre-push typecheck, unit-test,
OpenAPI, and formatting gate also passed.
- The repository-wide `PYTHON=.venv/bin/python bash test.sh` run reached
and passed every Parakeet test, but was not green due unrelated
current-tree failures in desktop release contracts and fast-unit timing
guards; one unrelated memory-adapter process was interrupted after
remaining idle on a live Google connection for more than eight minutes.
- GitHub's backend unit, image-smoke, source-closure, API-contract,
sync-gauntlet, and listen-gauntlet checks passed. The broad hermetic E2E
job twice reproduced the current unrelated
`TestSurfaceDefaultAccessMatrix...[agent_tools]` failure (113 passed, 3
skipped); the identical failure is present on PR #10400, whose diff only
touches sync cloud-task files.

The real CUDA concurrency trigger was not reproduced on a development
GPU. Its production recurrence and removal by the live configuration
rollout provide the runtime evidence.

## Tests

-
`test_exact_incident_error_transitions_real_worker_health_to_unhealthy`
covers the incident error flowing through the real worker state into
HTTP 503 health.
- `TestGPUWorkerFatalCUDA` covers fatal classification, fail-closed
admission, callback idempotency, thread shutdown, ordinary error
recovery, and OOM separation.
- RNNT tests cover fatal startup/decode propagation and the nonfatal
fallback path.
- Helm tests require CUDA graphs off plus HTTP readiness/liveness
probes.
- Monitoring contract tests require both per-pod alerts and application
status labels.

## Failure class (fixes)

Failure-Class: new

## Failure-class transition narrative (only when needed)

This adds `FC-poisoned-accelerator-readiness`. Violated contract: a
serving GPU process whose CUDA context is unsafe must not remain Ready
or accept more inference. The canonical guard is the GPU worker's fatal
state, surfaced through `/health` to Kubernetes readiness/liveness, with
CUDA graphs prohibited when batch and streaming inference share a
process. Evidence is the 2026-07-23 production incident, its recurrence
on replacement pod `prod-omi-parakeet-6cf8b5ccbf-d5786`, and the
enabling configuration from PR #7938.

## New guards (only when adding a check or ratchet)

The Helm and monitoring contract checks would have caught the 2026-07-23
Parakeet incident configuration and observability gaps. This is not a
shared primitive because CUDA-context validity and Parakeet's mixed
batch/streaming decoder configuration are specific to this GPU service.

## Scoped cleanups (optional)

The Parakeet dashboard's HTTP-style `status=~"[45].."` filters were
changed to the application's actual `status="error"` label.

ci(desktop): poll every 15 min so beta candidates cut reliably (#10414)

## Summary
Make desktop beta candidates cut **reliably** by polling every 15
minutes instead of hourly.

## Why they weren't auto-triggering
The `push` trigger fires correctly on every macOS merge (verified:
recent `push` auto-release runs all succeed), but it fires
**immediately** on merge — at which point the change is still inside the
60s quiet window AND its required CI checks (`Desktop Swift Build &
Tests`, `Release Compile`, `Release Eligibility`, ~10-20 min) have not
completed, so the planner fail-closes ("check missing"/"quiet window")
and declines. The only re-evaluation after CI turns green was the
**hourly** backstop — far too infrequent to reliably land in the window
where all three checks are green and the build fence is clear. Net:
candidates almost never auto-cut.

## Fix
`schedule` cron `17 * * * *` → `*/15 * * * *`. The push trigger stays as
the immediate attempt; the 15-min backstop re-evaluates shortly after a
change's CI settles. Builds stay serialized by the one-active-release
fence (Codemagic build status on the latest tag), so a frequent poll
cannot pile up builds — it just cuts the newest eligible candidate when
one is ready.

## Verification
- `pytest` plan + flow-contract → 27 passed, 37 subtests. actionlint
clean. AGENTS.md updated.

ci(desktop): poll every 15 min so candidates cut reliably after CI settles

The push trigger fires immediately on every macOS merge, but at that instant the
change is inside the quiet window AND its required CI checks (Build & Tests /
Release Compile / Release Eligibility, ~10-20 min) are not yet green, so the
planner fail-closes and declines. The only post-CI re-evaluation was the hourly
backstop — too infrequent to reliably catch the window where all checks are
green and the build fence is clear, so candidates almost never auto-cut.

Change the schedule cron 17 * * * * -> */15 * * * *. The push trigger stays as
the immediate attempt; the 15-min backstop re-evaluates once a change's CI
settles. Builds remain serialized by the one-active-release fence, so frequent
polling cannot pile up builds.

fix(desktop): repair chat outbox revisions after restart (#10412)

## What this fixes

Omi Beta 113 can leave a backend turn-outbox row with the old payload
revision
when startup recovery terminalizes an interrupted chat turn. The outbox
drain
then throws on that oldest row every second and rolls back the whole
batch,
starving every later chat-sync row.

Startup recovery now repairs active outbox rows from the same canonical
turn
projection used by normal journal writes. Empty recovered terminal turns
become
explicit tombstones, while delivered history is left untouched.

## Product invariants affected

- INV-AGENT-*
- INV-CHAT-1

Both are preserved. `conversation_turns` remains the sole canonical
transcript,
and its backend outbox remains an atomic downstream projection. This
change
repairs that projection during the existing kernel-owned startup
transaction; it
does not add another lifecycle or transcript owner.

Failure-Class: FC-split-mutation-authority

- Focused journal/store regression suite: 69/69 passed.
- Agent logic harness, cross-surface smoke, and continuity gauntlet
passed.
- A read-only copy of the affected Beta 113 database repaired exactly
one
poisoned row, preserved delivered history, and drained the six later
rows.
- Named app: graceful restart restored auth/runtime readiness and a real
visible
  main-chat reply; no outbox revision errors.
- Stopped the requested comparison early to land: fixed build 26/29
fully
correct with 0 revision errors; Beta 113 0/12 fully correct because none
of
its successful UI turns reached durable delivery, while 366 more
revision
  errors accumulated.

fix(desktop): stop voice fence busy-looping agent bridge when signed out (#10406)

## What this fixes

A signed-out (or session-invalidated) desktop client can spin the
agent-bridge
startup path at full speed, emitting tens of thousands of
`Failed to start agent bridge: Please sign in to use AI chat` errors per
minute
and bloating the local log to multiple gigabytes while burning CPU. One
observed
session produced **~56,000 of these errors in about a minute**.

## The issue (root cause)

The voice/realtime "persistence fence" retries a kernel context snapshot
in a
tight loop:

```
RealtimeHubController.refreshVoiceContextAfterPersistenceFence()
  while !Task.isCancelled {
    ...
    guard await refreshVoiceContextSnapshot() else {
      if Task.isCancelled { return false }
      continue          // ← retried on EVERY failure, with no backoff
    }
    ...
  }
```

`refreshVoiceContextSnapshot()` returns `false` for **two very
different**
situations, and the loop treated them identically:

1. **Transient** — a newer write/turn landed across an `await`; retrying
is correct.
2. **Terminal** — the owner signed out or the session was invalidated
mid-turn.
The kernel snapshot can now *never* resolve, because the agent bridge
refuses
   to start without a current authorization
(`ChatProvider.ensureBridgeStarted` → `captureAuthorizationSnapshot() ==
nil`
   → `presentBridgeStartupFailure(.authMissing)`).

The terminal case is what makes it pathological: the auth-missing check
returns
**instantly** (no network round-trip), so the loop iterates thousands of
times
per second. (Signed-in snapshot failures are naturally rate-limited by
their
network call, which is why this only melts down while signed out.)

Each iteration emitted one `Failed to start agent bridge` and one
`retaining the last voice context after an unresolved kernel snapshot`,
matching
the 1:1 interleave seen in the logs.

## The fix

Gate the retry on ownership. The loop now captures its owner scope at
entry and
only retries while it still owns that **authenticated** scope, via a new
pure
policy:

```swift
RealtimeHubLifecyclePolicy.canRetryPersistenceFence(
  taskCancelled: Bool,
  fenceOwnerScope: RealtimeHubOwnerScope,
  currentOwnerScope: RealtimeHubOwnerScope
) -> Bool
// false if cancelled, if currently signed out, or if the owner changed
```

On a terminal condition the fence returns `false` (setting
`pendingSessionRefreshReason` so a later valid session can resume)
instead of
busy-looping. Transient retries under the same authenticated owner are
unchanged.

## Tests

New `RealtimeHubPersistenceFencePolicyTests` covers the decision table:
retry while owner unchanged + authenticated; **stop** on cancel, on
sign-out
mid-fence (the exact trigger), on owner swap, and while signed out. The
policy is
a pure function, so this is behavioral coverage without standing up the
whole
controller.

## Product invariants

Touches path globs for **INV-CHAT-1** (one shared transcript across
surfaces)
and **INV-VOICE-1** (one desktop voice-turn lifecycle owner). Both are
**preserved**: this change only alters the persistence-fence retry
*termination*
condition. It adds no lifecycle owner, does not touch
`VoiceTurnCoordinator` /
the reducer, and does not touch the chat write-path or transcript, so
their
guard tests are unchanged.

Failure-Class: none

## Verification

- `swift-format` clean on all changed files.
- New unit test passes: `swift test --filter
RealtimeHubPersistenceFencePolicyTests`.
- Fix is contained to the fence retry decision; transient-retry behavior
under a
  stable authenticated owner is unchanged.

ci(desktop): qualification fails open on infra, closed on genuine failure (#10408)

## Summary
Make the desktop beta qualification **fail open on infra, fail closed on
genuine failure**. If a qualification machine actually ran the gate and
the build **failed**, promotion is blocked (a bad build must never reach
Beta). But if the machines are **down / unresponsive / broke before
qualifying**, that must not block the release — it still ships to
Codemagic/Sparkle/Beta. This removes the single-machine SPOF that
stalled beta releases all week.

## Change
Each lane (`codemagic-lane`, `qualify-m1-studio`, `qualify-m4-mini`) now
emits a `lane_verdict`:
- `qualified` — ran qualification and published immutable evidence.
- `failed` — the qualification step RAN and returned failure (genuinely
bad build).
- `unavailable` — the qualification step never ran (runner offline,
skipped, or a setup failure before it), so infra could not produce a
verdict.

The `verdict` job (the only job that can fail the run; all lanes are
`continue-on-error`):
1. any lane `failed` → **exit 1** (block promotion),
2. else any lane `qualified` → exit 0 (promote),
3. else (all `unavailable`) → **exit 0, fail open** (unreachable
machines don't block).

## Scope / caveat (honest)
The distinction uses the qualification **step outcome** (ran-and-failed
vs never-ran), so it does not modify the contested
`qualify-desktop-beta.sh`. It correctly fails open for the cases you
asked about — machines offline/unresponsive/setup-failed. An infra
failure that happens *inside* the qualification run (e.g. a mid-run
timeout, the cold-build-timeout being fixed separately in #10374) still
classifies as `failed`/blocking; cleanly separating that would require
the qualify script to emit a structured passed/failed/errored verdict —
recommended as a coordinated follow-up.

## Verification
- `pytest` flow-contract + plan suites → 27 passed, 37 subtests.
actionlint clean. `check-release-process-guards.py` passes. AGENTS.md
updated.

ci(desktop): remove self-hosted pre-tag-readiness gate before candidate creation (#10405)

## Summary
Complete the continuous-deployment model: **create the beta candidate on
every macOS change gated only by the hosted CI checks, then qualify it**
— by removing the self-hosted `pre-tag-readiness` gate that sat *before*
candidate creation.

## Why
`tag-release` previously had `needs: [plan-release, pre-tag-readiness]`
and consumed a readiness-evidence artifact, so an immutable candidate
tag could not be created until a job ran on the self-hosted trusted-M1
runner. That was:
- a **single point of failure** (a down/busy runner blocked ALL releases
— the root of this week's release stalls), and
- contrary to "create the release on every change, then qualify it" (it
qualified *before* the candidate existed).

Candidate creation is already gated by the planner's exact-SHA hosted CI
checks (`Release Eligibility`, `Desktop Swift Build & Tests`, `Desktop
Swift Release Compile`), and the heavy validation runs *after* the
candidate in `desktop_qualify_beta.yml` (Codemagic primary +
`qualify-m1-studio`/`qualify-m4-mini`). A candidate that fails
qualification is simply never promoted to Beta, so the pre-candidate
self-hosted gate was redundant safety.

## Changes
- `desktop_auto_release.yml`: removed the `pre-tag-readiness` job and
`tag-release`'s dependency + evidence download/verify steps.
`tag-release` now `needs: [plan-release]`.
- `test_plan_desktop_release.py`: replaced the
readiness-workflow-contract test with one asserting candidate creation
has no self-hosted pre-tag gate.
- `desktop/macos/scripts/pre-tag-readiness.sh` /
`verify-pre-tag-readiness.py` retained for local readiness use (no
longer in the release critical path).

## Verification
- `pytest` plan + flow-contract → 27 passed, 37 subtests. actionlint
clean. `check-release-process-guards.py` passes. Jobs now `plan-release`
+ `tag-release`; `tag-release needs: [plan-release]`.

fix(release): pre-tag readiness gate crashed on bash 3.2, blocking every auto-release (#10402)

## Summary
Fix the bug that has been silently blocking every desktop auto-release.
`pre-tag-readiness.sh` runs on the trusted M1 under **macOS bash 3.2**,
and line 205 expanded `"${KEEP_FLAG[@]}"` — an EMPTY array under `set
-u`, which traps `unbound variable` on bash 3.2 and exits 1. `KEEP_FLAG`
is empty on the normal path (`KEEP_STACK != 1`), so the readiness gate
failed on effectively every release, and `tag-release` (which `needs:
pre-tag-readiness`) skipped → no tag, no GitHub release, no Codemagic
build. This is why scheduled/auto releases produced no tags and I had to
hand-tag 111–114.

## Fix
Use the bash-3.2-safe `${KEEP_FLAG[@]+"${KEEP_FLAG[@]}"}` expansion
(only expands when set). Behaves identically on bash 4.4+.

## Verification
- Reproduced on `/bin/bash` 3.2.57 (the M1 runner's bash): `set -u;
KEEP_FLAG=(); "${KEEP_FLAG[@]}"` → `unbound variable`, exit 1. The
`[@]+` form → no error, empty→`[--readiness]`, populated→`[--readiness
--keep-stack]`.
- `bash -n` clean. Failing live evidence: run 30022355954,
`pre-tag-readiness` step `KEEP_FLAG[@]: unbound variable` at line 205.
- Regression guard added to `test_pre_tag_readiness_workflow_contract`
(forbids the unsafe form, requires the safe one). `pytest` plan suite →
6 passed.

Failure-Class: new

fix(setup): resolve repo root without a work tree in linked worktrees (#10401)

`git rev-parse --show-toplevel` exits 128 ("this operation must be run in
a work tree") in a linked worktree whose git context resolves to a git
dir rather than a work tree (e.g. a Conductor workspace). Three call
sites assumed it always returns a path:

- Makefile PYTHON collapsed to an empty prefix, breaking every target
  with `/scripts/dev-harness/_resolve_python.sh: No such file`.
- install-git-hooks.sh aborted `make setup` at setup-hooks (Error 128).
- The installed pre-commit/pre-push dispatcher aborted the hook, forcing
  `git push --no-verify` and silently bypassing the local gate.

Makefile and the dispatcher fall back to `pwd` (make and git hooks both
run from the top of the work tree) while keeping the root computed
in-shell, so a checkout path with quote/`$` chars still cannot inject.
install-git-hooks.sh resolves its root from the script's own location.
Adds a regression to scripts/test-make-setup.sh that reproduces the
128 condition via a bare GIT_DIR and fails on the pre-fix Makefile.

Failure-Class: FC-shell-source-path-interpolation

fixes #10352
backend: cover main-queue routing and the backfill lane sizing

ci: size the sync backfill lane so dispatch cannot outrun the worker

Dispatch concurrency now matches the worker's instance ceiling, a warm instance
keeps a scale-from-zero poke from being rejected outright, and retry backoff is
bounded so one transient rejection cannot strand a recording for hours.

backend: send every sync job to the main queue

An offline recording can never carry server capture proof, because the server
was not in the loop when it was captured. Every offline upload therefore
classifies as backfill, which routed the entire offline workload to a lane
provisioned for occasional historical recovery: a worker admitting four jobs at
once behind a queue offering four at a time.

Cloud Run rejected the surplus with no available instance, and Cloud Tasks
retried each rejection with exponential backoff up to an hour, so a recording
could sit scheduled almost a day out while the workers were idle. Recordings
uploaded fine and then never became conversations.

The lane label stays on the payload for metering and reporting; it no longer
selects the queue.

Route DELETE /v1/personas/{persona_id} to delete_persona instead of the thumbnail uploader (#10039)

The route decorator is stacked on the wrong function:

    @router.delete('/v1/personas/{persona_id}', tags=['v1'], response_model=AppThumbnailUploadResponse)
    @router.post('/v1/app/thumbnails', tags=['v1'], response_model=AppThumbnailUploadResponse)
    @max_part_size(APP_IMAGE_MAX_PART_SIZE)
    async def upload_app_thumbnail_endpoint(file: UploadFile = File(...), uid: str = Depends(auth.get_current_user_uid)):

so DELETE /v1/personas/{persona_id} is served by the thumbnail uploader. Thirty-four lines below,
the function that was meant to serve it carries no decorator at all:

    def delete_persona(persona_id: str, secret_key: str = Header(...)):

Three consequences, all live on main:

- No persona can be deleted through the API. The request reaches a handler that requires a
  multipart file body and then uploads a thumbnail; delete_persona_db is never called.
- delete_persona is unreachable dead code.
- The admin gate went with it. delete_persona checks secret_key against ADMIN_KEY and 403s
  otherwise; the handler actually serving the route authenticates with a regular per-user
  Firebase uid, so the route's declared auth and its real auth disagree.

The response_model was wrong for the same reason: a delete was documented as returning
AppThumbnailUploadResponse. It now uses AppMutationResponse, which is `{status: str}` and matches
both what delete_persona returns and what the sibling DELETE /v1/apps/{app_id} already declares.

Moving the decorator restores the intended dispatch without touching either handler's body. The
POST /v1/app/thumbnails route the decorator was stacked on is unaffected.

The path is not present in docs/api-reference/app-client-openapi.json, so no published client
contract moves and no spec regeneration is needed.

Tests (tests/unit/test_persona_delete_route_dispatch.py) read the registered route table off the
router and assert which endpoint each path resolves to, so they fail if the decorator ever drifts
onto the wrong function again rather than checking source text:
- DELETE /v1/personas/{persona_id} resolves to delete_persona, and to exactly one route
- the served handler still takes secret_key and does not require a file upload
- POST /v1/app/thumbnails still resolves to upload_app_thumbnail_endpoint
- the thumbnail uploader serves no DELETE route at all

  methods=['DELETE']), while the thumbnail POST test passes both ways. Re-applied: 4 passed

fix(people): 404 instead of 500 when renaming a missing person (#9480)

* fix(people): 404 instead of 500 when renaming a missing person

PATCH /v1/users/people/{person_id}/name -> update_person did a bare Firestore
.update(), which raises NotFound on a missing/stale person id (for example a
person already removed via the idempotent DELETE, still referenced by another
device or a retry). The router had no existence check and no handler, so it
surfaced as HTTP 500 instead of 404.

update_person now checks existence and returns False; the endpoint raises 404 when
it does. This mirrors the sibling get_single_person (already 404s via get_person)
and the repo convention in database/advice.py, where update_advice guards the same
.update() call against NotFound.

Verification: python -m pytest tests/unit/test_update_person_missing.py -> 2 passed
(missing -> False + no .update(); existing -> True + .update()). The router 404/200
mapping is covered in tests/routers/test_users.py. check_module_stub_pollution -> 0.

* fix(people): also 404 on the NotFound race in update_person

Addresses review feedback: the existence check alone still left the Firestore
NotFound -> 500 path open when the person doc is deleted between the .exists read
and .update(). Catch google.api_core.exceptions.NotFound around the update and
return False so that race 404s too, matching the check-plus-catch pattern in
database/advice.py::update_advice. Adds a regression test that makes .update()
raise NotFound after a passing existence check.

Verification: python -m pytest tests/unit/test_update_person_missing.py -> 3 passed.
black + pyright clean; check_module_stub_pollution -> 0.

* test(users): harden update_person test against google-namespace stub pollution

The full-suite CI run (triggered by updating this branch onto main) failed
test_update_person_missing.py with ModuleNotFoundError: No module named
'google.api_core' — another test in the suite replaces the `google` namespace with a
stub at module scope, so this file's `from google.api_core.exceptions import NotFound`

fix(listen): meter custom-STT speech in an isolated fair-use lane (#10127)

* fix(listen): meter custom-STT speech in an isolated fair-use lane

Custom-STT sessions are exempt from transcription billing and live
caps by design — but _flush_usage returned early for them before any
recording, so their speech never reached the fair-use meter at all,
while everything downstream (conversation structuring, memory
extraction) still runs on Omi-paid LLM post-processing with no
accounting (#7690).

The speech now records into a new 'custom_stt' fair-use lane: valid in
_VALID_SPEECH_SOURCES (an unknown source would coerce to 'realtime' and
gate exempt users), excluded from LIVE_SPEECH_SOURCES so live
enforcement never reads it — the same isolation contract sync_backfill
already uses. Transcription usage recording stays skipped: no billing
semantics change, nobody gets gated. Quantifying and capping the spend
(the issue's policy questions) read this lane and are deliberately
separate decisions.

Regression tests (3 fail on old code): the lane records under its own
Redis keys; the source is valid and outside live enforcement; a
custom-STT session's flush meters speech into the lane and only the
lane — recording transcription usage for one asserts a failure.

Addresses the accounting half of #7690; capping policy remains open
there.

Failure-Class: none

fix(app): group conversations by local calendar day, not raw UTC (#10198) (#10398)

started_at/created_at arrive as UTC (ISO-8601 Z) and are kept UTC, but the
conversations list bucketed into day-groups by their raw UTC year/month/day
while the date filter used the local selectedDate. For any UTC+ viewer an
early-morning-local conversation was filed under the previous (UTC) day, so it
vanished from the "Today" group even though the local-day filter still found it.

Bucket by the local calendar day via a shared conversationLocalDayKey helper at
every grouping site; sorts (tz-independent ordering) stay on the raw timestamps.

Fixes #10198.

Co-authored-by: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix(sync): recover stuck offline recordings and enumerate current-firmware devices (#10397) Closes #10033. Closes #10197. Offline recordings upload and then sit at "Uploaded · processing on Omi" for 12+ hours without becoming conversations, some never sync at all, and stuck recordings never retry. Three independent causes, each fixed here. ## 1. Stuck sync jobs never self-healed (backend) A job whose worker died stayed in `processing` until the 24h reconcile TTL, so the client polled a job that would never finish. `get_sync_job` used to finalize any `processing` job quiet past `STALE_THRESHOLD_SECONDS` (600s) to `failed`, on every read and for every dispatch mode. That finalizer was later moved to the status-poll endpoint and gated on `dispatch_mode == 'cloud_tasks'`, which left inline/BYOK jobs with no self-heal at all and made every other job depend on a client actively polling. Restored the original behaviour. The pipeline heartbeats `updated_at` at every stage and per segment, so 600s of silence only ever means the worker is gone. `queued` jobs stay exempt — no worker has claimed them, and failing them caused the spurious "retrying" loops in #7469. This fixes the reported symptom end to end: the client already reverts a WAL from `uploaded` to `miss` and re-uploads when the server reports `failed`, so a dead job now recovers in ~10 minutes instead of 24 hours. ## 2. Ring-buffer devices were never enumerated in auto-sync (app) Background and auto-sync discovery read `firmwareRevision` off the raw connect object, which is frequently still `'Unknown'`. `isRingBufferFirmware('Unknown')` is false, so devices on firmware 3.0.20 and up — the current firmware — were routed to the multi-file enumerator, which returns nothing for them. Their offline recordings were never enumerated, never uploaded, and never became conversations, unless the user happened to open the Auto Sync page, the one caller that already passed the enriched firmware. `setDevice` now carries the firmware resolved by `getDeviceInfo`, and both discovery paths prefer it, falling back to the raw value so behaviour is never worse than before. ## 3. Offline backlogs drained a few files at a time (app) The backfill lane uploaded three files per batch, so recovering a backlog crawled. Both lanes now share one batch limit of twenty. The two lanes are kept. They earn their keep in `SyncUploadGate`, which holds a separate rate-limit domain per lane so a backfill cooldown cannot stall a freshly captured recording. They were not buying throughput control: ordering is newest-first regardless of lane, and the backend caps multipart part size (200 MB), not file count — the fresh lane has been sending twenty per batch all along. ## Tests - `test_sync_job_stale_self_heal.py` — stale `processing` self-heals for inline and cloud_tasks, a fresh job is untouched, and a stale `queued` job is never finalized. - `wal_syncs_firmware_routing_test.dart` — the enriched firmware wins over a raw `'Unknown'`, falls back when absent, and a 3.0.20 device now classifies as ring. - `local_wal_sync_test.dart` — a small backlog drains in one batch; larger ones are bounded by the shared limit, newest first. - Existing sync-job tests updated to the restored self-heal contract. `cd backend && pytest` on the sync suites — 193 passed. `cd app && bash test.sh` — 933 passed. `bash scripts/analyze_ratchet.sh` — passed. Failure-Class: none

app: apply const constructors across the app (451 → 0) (#10396)

## What

Applies the const-family lints across the app with `dart fix`, one
commit per
file, and locks the floor at zero in the analyzer ratchet baseline.

Cleared to zero:
- `prefer_const_constructors`: 451 → 0
- `prefer_const_declarations`: 25 → 0
- `prefer_const_literals_to_create_immutables`: 8 → 0

331 fixes across 93 files (86 in `lib`, 6 in `test`/`integration_test`).
Adding
`const` lets Flutter skip rebuilding and re-allocating these
widgets/objects.
Split one file per commit so each change is a small, self-contained,
keyword-only diff.

## How it was produced

`dart fix --apply` for the three const codes, plus `unnecessary_const`
to clean
up the 2 redundant child `const`s that fixing the parents exposed. Every
change
was verified to be a pure `const`/`final`/`var`→`const` edit — the diff
is
identical modulo the `const` keyword and whitespace, nothing else.

## Baseline

The ratchet baseline drops **only** the three const entries, so future
const
violations fail CI. I deliberately did **not** run `--update-baseline`
wholesale: it also lowered `depend_on_referenced_packages` (4→0) and
`unnecessary_import` (12→11) from this machine's analyzer run, which
under-reports those relative to CI — locking them in would make CI fail
when
they reappear. Those baselines are left untouched.

## Verification

- `dart analyze`: 0 remaining const-family findings.
- `scripts/analyze_ratchet.sh`: passes.
- `bash test.sh`: 928 tests pass.

fix(backend): stop discarding chat answers that streamed before a bounded stop (#10305) ## Problem `execute_agentic_chat_stream` persists its result in two steps: it sets `callback_data['answer']`, then falls through to `yield None` — the terminal sentinel the router waits for: ```python if callback_data is not None: callback_data['answer'] = ''.join(full_response) # line 842 ... except asyncio.TimeoutError: ... yield f'error: {AGENT_STREAM_TIMEOUT_MESSAGE}' return # skips both ... yield None # Signal completion ``` Both early-exit paths `return` before either step. In `routers/chat.py`, `answered` only becomes true when a falsy chunk arrives *and* `callback_data['answer']` is set, so on a bounded stop `answered` stays false and `emit_stream_error_fallback` writes a canned error in its place. The result: a user watches a complete answer stream in, sees it replaced by "The response took too long", and finds nothing in their chat history — while `full_response` held the full text the entire time. This is not a rare path. The code's own comment notes "some app tools allow 120s", so a two-tool answer can legitimately exceed the 150s cap — exactly the long, expensive answers that hurt most to lose. ## Change Both the deadline and failure paths now keep what was streamed and emit the sentinel so the router persists it. Citation sources are carried over too, so `[1]`-style references inside that text still resolve instead of being stripped as out-of-range. When nothing was streamed there is nothing to keep, so the terminal error still stands and the fallback behaves exactly as before. `callback_data['error']` is still recorded on both paths, so the failure remains observable server-side. ## Tests `tests/unit/test_chat_async_offload.py` — the shared harness now exposes `callback_data`: - **keeps an answer streamed before the deadline** — verified to fail on current code with `assert 'error: The response took too long…' is None`, i.e. the answer was discarded. - **keeps an answer streamed before a producer crash** — fails on current code with the equivalent failure-path assertion. - **still errors when nothing was streamed** — passes before and after, pinning the no-partial-answer behaviour so this cannot widen into swallowing real failures. 13/13 in that file, 32/32 with the chat quota and vision stream suites. `scripts/scan_async_blockers.py`: 0 findings. `black` clean. ## Follow-ups (not in this PR) - The file and persona routes in `utils/retrieval/graph.py` drop partial answers the same way, but structurally differently — their answer comes from `await task` rather than an accumulated buffer, so it needs its own fix. - `first_event_deadline` is computed *before* the 25s setup block, so a slow cold setup can leave ~1s for the first token and kill a healthy stream. Failure-Class: none

refactor(desktop/ptt): emit recovery metadata and capture-start-fail lifecycle before terminal

Address two P2 review threads from Codex:

1. Recovery metadata ordering (line 1163): when a silent hub turn triggers
   a capture rebuild, recoveryTriggered was called inside
   requestCoreAudioCaptureRecovery — after terminate already emitted the
   snapshot. The triggering turn was recorded with recovery_triggered=false
   and no recovery_attempt_id, so the next-turn recovery outcome could not
   be joined back. Now recoveryTriggered is called before terminate, and
   requestCoreAudioCaptureRecovery gets a recoveryAlreadyTriggered flag to
   avoid minting a second correlation id.

2. Capture-start-fail lifecycle (line 2144): when startCapture threw, the
   reducer terminal effect (performTerminalCleanup) never called terminate,
   so capture_start_outcome=failed never produced a lifecycle snapshot.
   Now terminate is called in the catch block so the exact failure scenario
   this PR exists to diagnose is observable.

Thread 3 (empty live transcript termination, line 1517) was already
addressed in commit e8c98b8 — the else branch calls terminate.

  branch and the batch-transcription catch) so each finalization resolves the
  recorder exactly once. Recovery resolution uses the recorder's tracked
  first-usable-frame instead of the sometimes-unavailable peak-based isNearZero,

test(desktop/ptt): cover capture-lifecycle classification and redaction

PTTAttemptLifecycleRecorderTests (14) drive the production API with an
injected clock and verify all four failure classifications, the recovery
correlation (join on recovery_attempt_id; a non-judgeable turn does not
consume a pending recovery), timing buckets, static classification
precedence, peak-amplitude reduction, and that every emitted field is a

DesktopDiagnosticsManagerTests gains an end-to-end test asserting the

and no raw identity.

feat(desktop/ptt): add privacy-bounded capture-lifecycle diagnostics

Add a per-PTT-attempt lifecycle correlation model that classifies a silent
capture failure into one of four causally distinct boundaries the existing
late ptt_audio_capture_silent_turn snapshot cannot locate:

  1. capture_never_operational  (start failed / async start race, no callback)
  2. zero_or_near_zero_samples   (operational but only zero frames)
  3. released_before_usable_audio (key up before first usable frame)
  4. recovery_outcome_*          (rebuild did/did-not restore next judgeable turn)

PTTAttemptLifecycleRecorder accumulates the capture boundaries that already
exist (press, capture-start requested/accepted/failed, first audio callback,
first usable frame, release/disposition, recovery) into one redacted snapshot
emitted as ptt_audio_capture_lifecycle at termination. Fields are bounded,
low-cardinality buckets/classes only — never raw audio, device names, hardware
IDs, paths, OSStatus, or error strings. Raw PCM is reduced to an energy bucket
while searching for the first usable frame, then scanning stops.

A recovery requested on attempt N is resolved by the next judgeable attempt N+1
(recovery_attempt_id join; a non-judgeable turn does not consume it). Routes
through DesktopDiagnosticsManager into the existing ring buffer + Sentry
incident attachment; remote (PostHog) only for non-committed terminations.

Wired into PushToTalkManager at the existing seams and AudioCaptureService
(onInputRouteChanged for the route-change flag). Product behavior and capture/
recovery semantics unchanged; silence gates and silent-audio provider routing
untouched.

Motivated by the #9081 dead-mic incident cluster (resolved by #9770); closes
the residual capture-boundary observability gap that cluster left out of scope.

SCA-71: fix chat-agent gateway capability mismatch (#10385) ## Summary - Fix the gateway-only `chat_agent` Anthropic Messages route by removing its injected `effort: medium` provider option. - The route adapter merges provider options into every request after replacing the caller model with `route.chat_agent.model_config.001`; Anthropic rejected the resulting streaming request with HTTP 400 before output. The direct chat-agent client does not send that option. - Keep the gateway-specific model, native Anthropic surface, transport-only fallback policy, circuit behavior, accounting, and bounded metric labels unchanged. - This branch is rebased onto the emergency revert in #10384. Production Cloud Run gateway mode remains source-controlled `off`; this PR does not enable a caller, deploy, or route traffic. ## Ranked hypotheses and evidence 1. **Confirmed:** the generated route's Anthropic-only `effort` parameter was injected into the exact chat-agent Messages streaming shape and caused a simulated provider capability 400. Removing it makes the same request complete its `message_stop` stream. 2. **Ruled out at this boundary:** a generic Anthropic transport error would invoke the existing direct-provider fallback; an HTTP 4xx remains a non-transport terminal error and intentionally does not silently fall back. 3. **Not changed:** the `claude-sonnet-5` gateway model differs from the direct `claude-sonnet-4-6` profile, but the deterministic reproduction isolates the failing capability to `effort`; no model-name change is needed for this fix. ## RED / GREEN evidence - RED before the configuration change: `source backend/.venv/bin/activate && pytest -q backend/tests/unit/test_llm_gateway_anthropic_passthrough.py -k unsupported_effort_option` — 1 failed: the exact streaming request returned HTTP 400 and recorded `provider_4xx` before output. - GREEN after the change: `source backend/.venv/bin/activate && pytest -q backend/tests/unit/test_llm_gateway_anthropic_passthrough.py backend/tests/unit/test_llm_gateway_config.py backend/tests/unit/test_gateway_anthropic_client.py` — 43 passed. - `make preflight` — passed (19 deterministic checks). ## Rollout verification and rollback - Keep the production caller flags off. In a separately approved non-production activation, send the authenticated `omi:auto:chat-agent` Anthropic Messages tool stream and verify a terminal `message_stop`, no `provider_4xx` / `capability_mismatch`, unchanged accounting labels, and unchanged fallback/circuit telemetry. - Roll back by reverting this PR; no live state is changed by this PR. Failure-Class: none

docs(agents): one CLAUDE.md, resolvable pointers, and a ratchet on every AGENTS.md (#10346) ## Why An audit of every agent-influencing doc found four pointers that resolve to nothing, one rule that contradicts another, and a size ratchet guarding the smallest file while the ones it delegates to grew unbounded. Agents cannot tell a stale pointer from a live one. They follow it, waste a turn, and improvise — which is exactly what guidance exists to prevent. ## What changed ### One `CLAUDE.md`, not five Every component carried a `CLAUDE.md` whose entire content was "read `AGENTS.md`". Every rename had to be mirrored in two files, and the root pointer had already drifted — it advertised `desktop/CLAUDE.md` and `desktop/e2e/SKILL.md`, neither of which exists (both live under `desktop/macos/`). Now there is exactly one `CLAUDE.md`, at the root, telling agents to treat `AGENTS.md` as the instruction file and read the nearest one above their work. This also fixes a gap it wasn't aimed at: **`.github/AGENTS.md` had no `CLAUDE.md` sibling and appeared in no index, so it was never auto-loaded.** It holds deploy concurrency, immutable image tags, rollout waits, and `backend-secrets` ordering — the highest-consequence content in the repo. ### Removed the landing policy `Behavior` said *"never ask for confirmation, make decisions autonomously and proceed"* forty lines above `Safety Rules` saying *"never push or open PRs unless explicitly asked."* Read literally by a weak model, the first licenses what the second forbids. `Behavior` is now scoped to local, reversible work. The PR/merge authorization policy is gone entirely — whether to commit, push, open a PR, or merge is the user's workflow preference and the agent's judgment in context, not something the repository should decide. What remains is genuine repo mechanics: branch from current `main`, and merge without squashing so changes stay cleanly revertible. ### Repaired pointers that don't resolve | Doc | Claimed | Actual | |---|---|---| | `CLAUDE.md` | `desktop/CLAUDE.md`, `desktop/e2e/SKILL.md` | under `desktop/macos/` | | `app/AGENTS.md` | `lib/watch_interface.dart` "13 methods" | `lib/pigeon_interfaces.dart`, six APIs (watch recorder, BLE, Ray-Ban Meta) | | `app/AGENTS.md` | `lib/gen/flutter_communicator.g.dart` | `lib/gen/pigeon_communicator.g.dart` | | `app/AGENTS.md` | `ios/Runner/FlutterCommunicator.g.swift` | `ios/Runner/PigeonCommunicator.g.swift` | | `app/AGENTS.md` | `ios/Runner/PhoneCallsPlugin.swift` | `ios/Runner/PhoneCalls/OmiPhoneCallsPlugin.swift` | | `desktop/macos/AGENTS.md` | `.claude/skills/{firebase,sentry-release}/SKILL.md`, `.claude/settings.json` | agent-local, never in this repo | | `desktop/macos/AGENTS.md` | launch via `./reset-and-run.sh` | does not exist | ### Two checks so this cannot recur **`check_agent_doc_references.py`** — every repo path an agent doc points at must exist. Gitignored paths are exempt: build outputs and `.env` files are absent by design, and gitignore is the repo's own answer to "is this expected to be missing?", so no hand-maintained allowlist is needed. Bare filenames like `auth.py` are shorthand, not pointers, and are ignored — resolving those would need judgment, and a check that needs judgment is a check that acquires an allowlist. **`check_agents_md_lean.py`** now covers all seven `AGENTS.md` files instead of only root. Root was already bounded while the guides it delegates to were not — `desktop/macos/AGENTS.md` had reached 44.8KB, **2.9× the file the ratchet protected**. Budgets ratchet down, never up, and a new `AGENTS.md` with no budget fails, so no guide can land unbounded. Both are registered in `.github/checks-manifest.yaml` with `local` and `ci` lanes. ### Token cost `Formatting` duplicated `backend/AGENTS.md` verbatim and is moot anyway because the pre-commit hook formats staged files. `Documentation Maintenance` only applies when editing docs. Both were paid for in every session; both moved to `docs/agents/` behind an index row. Root `AGENTS.md`: **16,326 → 15,236 bytes (91% → 85% of budget), headroom up 65%.** ## Verification ``` python3 .github/scripts/check_agents_md_lean.py → ok: 7 AGENTS.md files within budget python3 .github/scripts/check_agent_doc_references.py → agent doc references OK python3 .github/scripts/test_run_checks.py → Ran 21 tests, OK scripts/pr-preflight --suggest → invariants: none; no fix: commits, no failure class required ``` Both new checks were mutation-tested against the real defects before the fixes landed: - appending 2,000 bytes to `desktop/macos/AGENTS.md` → `46792 bytes exceeds budget of 45500`, exit 1 - adding `` `backend/definitely_gone.py` `` to `web/admin/AGENTS.md` → `reference does not exist`, exit 1 - the checker's own run on unfixed `main` reported all four dead pointers listed above `make preflight` selects 51 checks for this diff. All pass except `desktop-beta-admission-firestore-contention`, which **fails identically on a clean `origin/main` worktree** in this environment — it needs the Firebase emulator toolchain (a JVM the local `java` shim can't provide). Verified by running it on a pristine `origin/main` worktree: same exit 1, before any of my changes. It is unrelated to this diff (no overlap with `desktop_update_channels.py`, `backend/testing/desktop_beta_admission/**`, or `package*.json`); it is selected only because `.github/checks-manifest.yaml` is in its triggers. CI provides the toolchain. ## Not in scope The e2e `SKILL.md` files (32KB + 35KB) are deliberately left unratcheted — they are loaded on demand, not per session. 🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code) ## Follow-up: manifest trigger + budget recalibration Two focused CI/review follow-ups on top of the original diff: **Root `AGENTS.md` trigger (resolves review thread on `.github/checks-manifest.yaml:71`).** `fnmatch`/`PurePath.match` require a directory segment, so `**/AGENTS.md` never matches the root guide — a PR editing only `AGENTS.md` skipped both `agents-md-lean` and `agent-doc-references`. Added an explicit `AGENTS.md` entry alongside the recursive glob in both checks, locked by a trigger-matching regression test (`test_root_agents_md_selects_agent_doc_checks`). **Desktop guide byte budget.** `desktop/macos/AGENTS.md` was 44792 bytes when this PR set its 45500-byte budget; `main` has since grown it to 46121 with Codemagic-primary release-pipeline detail, so the merge ref (46195) exceeded the budget and `agents-md-lean` failed. Recalibrated the byte budget to 47000 to accommodate `main`'s legitimate content; the line budget (560) still holds and the other six guides remain well under budget.

fix(release): raise qualification cold-build timeout so fresh tags stop timing out (Beta unblock) (#10374) ## Problem — this is why Beta has been stuck since v0.12.103 Every fresh desktop candidate times out in qualification. Root cause, from run [29965341760](https://github.com/BasedHardware/omi/actions/runs/29965341760) (v0.12.111 on David's M1): ``` qualification failed: desktop launch not dispatched within 3600s ``` A cold, from-scratch rebuild of the exact tag compiles **~1190 SwiftPM modules** (FluidAudio, MarkdownUI, Firebase, ONNX, …) *before* the named bundle is even packaged, signed, installed, and launched. On a self-hosted M1 that cold build alone runs ~65 min and dispatches the launch at ~75 min — past the **3600s (60 min)** `DESKTOP_PREPARE_WAIT_SECS` budget. The build log ends at `[1139/1190] Compiling` right at the deadline. It doesn't self-heal: a timed-out build leaves an incomplete `.build` that the qualification swift-cache **fail-closes on**, so the warm retry rebuilds cold and times out again — an unbreakable loop. And the hourly auto-release cuts a new tag (superseding the old one) before any tag's retry can pass. Result: v0.12.99–v0.12.112 all failed qualification; Beta served v0.12.103 for a day. This is the **same boundary already bumped once** (1800s→3600s, run 29904736566, which stalled at `1107/1182`). 3600s was still short. ## Fix Raise the default cold-preparation budget to **5400s (90 min)**, overridable via `OMI_QUALIFY_PREPARE_WAIT_SECS`, covering the observed ~75-min cold path with headroom while staying under the self-hosted job cap. Warm same-SHA retries still finish in a fraction of this. The static timing-contract test is updated to the new default and records the recurrence history. Two-line functional change (`desktop/macos/scripts/qualify-desktop-beta.sh`) plus its contract test. Independent of #10363 (which hardens the *lanes*); this fixes the *time budget* every lane shares. It takes effect for tags cut after merge — so the next hourly `v*-macos` tag is the first that can actually qualify. **Note on the Codemagic lane:** CM's `max_build_duration` is 120 min, so a *cold* CM build (90-min prepare + install + T2) may exceed it; the self-hosted lanes are the reliable cold-build path under the at-least-one-passes verdict, and CM carries warm/incremental builds. Raising CM's budget is a follow-up (touches the same codemagic.yaml workflow as #10363). ## Verification - Reproduced the root cause from the live run log (`not dispatched within 3600s`, build at `[1139/1190]` at the 3600s mark, job window 23:11→00:24 = 73 min). - `bash -n desktop/macos/scripts/qualify-desktop-beta.sh` → OK - Value resolves as an integer for the `SECONDS + DESKTOP_PREPARE_WAIT_SECS` arithmetic; default 5400, `OMI_QUALIFY_PREPARE_WAIT_SECS` override honored. - `bash desktop/macos/tests/test-qualify-desktop-beta-contract.sh` → passed (asserts new 5400 default + 6300 combined bound) - `python3 .github/scripts/check-release-process-guards.py` → passed - `python3 .github/scripts/test_desktop_release_flow_contract.py` → 19 OK **UNVERIFIED until a post-merge tag runs:** that 5400s is actually sufficient end-to-end on the slowest registered runner. If a cold build ever exceeds 90 min, bump `OMI_QUALIFY_PREPARE_WAIT_SECS` on that runner — no code change needed. Failure-Class: new

fix(release): robust Codemagic qualification handoff + fail-open fallback gating (#10380) ## Two robustness defects in the merged qualification lanes (#10354/#10363) Cross-review found two real gaps in the multi-lane qualification, neither covered by the existing tests. Both keep the "at least one lane qualifies a healthy candidate" guarantee honest. ### 1. Codemagic artifact handoff was shape-fragile (and unverified end-to-end) The CM lane publishes `build/qualification/**` (JSON). Codemagic exposes recognized build outputs (`.app`/`.dmg`/`.zip`/…) as top-level artefacts but packages arbitrary files matching a directory/`**` glob into a **single zip artefact**. `desktop_codemagic_qualification.py` only matched a top-level `qualification-result.json`, so a zipped artefact would make the driver fail to find the result → **every CM qualification silently falls through to the self-hosted lanes**, defeating the CM-primary design. `fetch_result_artifact` now prefers a top-level file and otherwise **extracts the result from a zip artefact by basename** (so an internal path like `qualification/qualification-result.json` still resolves). The driver adapts to whatever shape Codemagic produces, so the digest-pinned `codemagic.yaml` needs no change. *Honesty note:* all prior live CM test builds died at the venv step (fixed separately in #10374), so a real CM build never produced this artefact — the handoff stays **UNVERIFIED end-to-end** until #10374 lets a CM qualification complete. This PR makes the driver correct for both artefact shapes and covers them with tests; the live confirmation is a follow-up once a green CM build exists. ### 2. `plan-fallbacks` did not fully fail open The `gh api` runner-status step fails open, but a failure in the **earlier token-generation step** fails the whole `plan-fallbacks` job with empty outputs. The lanes gated on `m{1,4}_online == 'true'`, so empty `!= 'true'` → **both self-hosted fallbacks skipped** — re-creating the single-machine outage on any token hiccup. Gate now fails open: `m{1,4}_online != 'false'` — a lane is skipped only when the planner **explicitly** reports the runner offline. A planner failure now lets the fallbacks attempt. ## Verification - `python3 .github/scripts/test_desktop_codemagic_qualification.py` → 13 OK (new: extract-from-zip, prefer-top-level-over-zip, zip-without-result-fails) - `python3 .github/scripts/test_desktop_release_flow_contract.py` → 20 OK (asserts `!= 'false'` fail-open gating on both lanes; `== 'true'` explicitly forbidden) - `actionlint .github/workflows/desktop_qualify_beta.yml` → exit 0 - `python3 .github/scripts/check-release-process-guards.py` → passed - `bash scripts/dev-harness/run-tests.sh` → 50 passed - `bash desktop/macos/tests/test-qualify-desktop-beta-contract.sh` → passed - `make preflight` → (see checks) Failure-Class: FC-single-machine-release-gate

fix(desktop): flip onboarding chatgpt/claude connect chip after browser oauth (#10375)

## Problem

In the Second Brain onboarding, clicking Connect on the ChatGPT or
Claude context row opens the provider's OAuth page in the browser, then
immediately checks the local connection status — before the user could
possibly have authorized. The check always reads "not connected",
silently resets the chip to "Connect", and nothing re-checks when the
user returns. The chip never flips to "✓ on".

A second, ChatGPT-specific defect surfaced during live testing: even
once the grant check runs, ChatGPT never verifies on non-prod (Beta/dev)
builds. The ChatGPT directory listing is a single global plugin that
always authorizes under `omi-chatgpt-prod`, but the app looked for the
env-switched `chatgptOAuthClientID` (`omi-chatgpt-dev` on non-prod), so
the grant never matched. Claude worked because its client ID is
hardcoded `omi-claude-prod`.

## Fix

The authoritative connection signal is the backend OAuth grant list
(`GET /v1/mcp/oauth/grants`).

- `MemoryExportService`: generalize
`refreshChatGPTDirectoryConnectionStatus()` into

fix(release): make Codemagic qualification handoff + fallback gating robust

Two defects found in the merged qualification lanes (#10354/#10363), neither
covered by the existing tests:

1. Codemagic artifact handoff was unverified and shape-fragile. The CM lane
   publishes build/qualification/** (JSON), and Codemagic packages arbitrary
   files matching a directory/** glob into a single zip artefact rather than
   exposing each as a top-level artefact. desktop_codemagic_qualification.py
   only matched a top-level 'qualification-result.json', so a zipped artefact
   would make every CM qualification silently fall through to the self-hosted
   lanes — defeating the CM-primary design. fetch_result_artifact now prefers
   the top-level file and otherwise extracts the result from a zip artefact by
   basename. Regression tests cover both shapes + zip-missing-result.

2. plan-fallbacks did not fully fail open. The gh-api status step fails open,
   but a failure in the earlier token-generation step fails the whole job with
   empty outputs, and the lanes gated on 'm{1,4}_online == true' → empty !=
   true → BOTH self-hosted fallbacks skipped, re-creating the single-machine
   outage on any token hiccup. Gate now fails open ('!= false'): a lane is
   skipped only when the planner explicitly reports the runner offline.

Verified: test_desktop_codemagic_qualification 13 OK (incl. zip cases);
test_desktop_release_flow_contract 20 OK (asserts fail-open gating);
actionlint exit 0; check-release-process-guards passed; dev-harness 50 passed;
qualification bootstrap contract passed.

Note: end-to-end CM handoff on a real green build stays UNVERIFIED until the
cold-build timeout fix (#10374) lets a CM qualification build complete; the
driver is now correct for whatever artefact shape Codemagic produces.

fix(release): harden desktop qualification lanes — independent machines, docker-daemon health, resilient venv (#10363) ## Follow-up hardening for the desktop qualification lanes (#10354) #10354 landed the Codemagic-primary + self-hosted-fallback qualification restructure and registered failure class `FC-single-machine-release-gate`. This PR repairs three gaps found by cross-review and by the first live Codemagic-lane run — the merged version does not yet deliver the "any independent machine can qualify" guarantee the class promises. Instance fixes for the same boundary. ### 1. Independent per-machine fallback lanes (cross-review finding) The merged version had a single `qualify` self-hosted job routed to any matching runner, so a sick-but-online David machine could absorb the job and Nik's mini would never try. Replaced with: - `plan-fallbacks` — reads runner online-status via the runners API (`m1_online`/`m4_online`); a failed query fails open. Gates each machine lane so an **offline** runner is skipped instead of queue-stalling the per-tag concurrency group (offline self-hosted jobs queue up to 24h). - `qualify-m1-studio` (label `omi-qual-m1-studio`) then `qualify-m4-mini` (label `omi-qual-m4-mini`) — one lane per physical machine (labels applied to both registered runners via the API). Serialized so the mini lane runs only when the M1 lane did not publish evidence; a sick-but-online machine can no longer absorb the other's attempt. Byte-identical steps after `steps:` (enforced by `test_desktop_release_flow_contract.py`). - `verdict` passes iff `CM_QUALIFIED || M1_QUALIFIED || M4_QUALIFIED`. ### 2. Docker-daemon health in Typesense selection (cross-review finding) `typesense_runtime()` auto-picked docker on `which docker` alone, so a docker CLI with a **dead daemon** sank the lane even with a native binary present. Now probes `docker info` (`_docker_daemon_healthy`, 15s timeout); a dead daemon falls through to native `typesense-server`, and `prerequisite_report` distinguishes "docker daemon" (present-but-dead) from "docker missing". ### 3. Resilient backend venv provisioning (live CM-lane failure) The first live Codemagic-lane test on **v0.12.111** (build `6a61554646f44fbd4070c8cf`) failed at the qualify step: ``` error: No download found for request: cpython-3.11.15-macos-aarch64-none make: *** [setup-backend] Error 2 ``` `make setup-backend` runs `uv python install 3.11.15` (exact patch), but a freshly `brew install`ed uv on the ephemeral CM Mac has a python-build-standalone index that lacks that exact patch — so the venv-provisioning step added in #10354 hard-broke the lane it was meant to enable. Now: try `make setup-backend` (exact-patch CI parity), and on failure fall back to a venv on the pinned **minor** version + `uv pip sync` of the exact platform lock. The lockfile still pins every dependency exactly; only the interpreter patch floats to what uv can fetch. ## Verification Local (all green): - Exercised the venv fallback against the real backend: `uv venv --python 3.11 + uv pip sync pylock.macos.toml` → `python -c "import uvicorn, fastapi"` OK. - `bash -n desktop/macos/scripts/qualify-desktop-beta.sh` → OK - `python3 .github/scripts/test_desktop_release_flow_contract.py` → 19 OK (incl. new multi-lane independence + byte-identical-lanes assertions) - `python3 -m pytest scripts/dev-harness/tests/` → 50 passed (incl. broken-daemon fallback + dead-daemon preflight) - `python3 .github/scripts/check-release-process-guards.py` → passed (post-rebase over #10358's codemagic.yaml change) - `bash desktop/macos/tests/test-qualify-desktop-beta-contract.sh` → passed - `actionlint .github/workflows/desktop_qualify_beta.yml` → exit 0 (custom runner labels registered in `.github/actionlint.yaml`; only the pre-existing SC2086 info remains) - `python3 .github/scripts/check_agents_md_lean.py` → within budget Live (release-infra exercised for real): - Both machine labels applied to the two registered runners; `mac-mini-m4-qualification` already executed a real qualification job end-to-end in #10354's verification. - After merge I will re-run the Codemagic lane on the newest valid candidate to confirm the venv fallback carries it through T2 on the ephemeral Mac. **UNVERIFIED until that run passes** — the fix is proven at the unit/component level (fallback venv imports backend deps) but not yet through a full green CM T2. - Separately, the self-hosted lane is qualifying **v0.12.111** (the real beta-unblock candidate, carrying the #10350 crash fix) on David's M1 via the pre-merge tag script; that run is the operator's actual beta unblock and is independent of this PR. ci(desktop): cut auto-release quiet window to 60s for prompt per-merge builds (#10376)

## Summary
Cut the desktop auto-release quiet window from **10 minutes to 60
seconds** so a macOS-affecting merge to main is tagged (GitHub release →
Codemagic) within ~a minute of landing, instead of waiting ten. Combined
with the push trigger (#10370/#10371), every macOS change now flows to a
Codemagic build promptly.

## Why it's safe
The one-active-release fence keys off the **Codemagic build status** on
the latest tag, so while a build runs (~78 min) rapid bursts already
collapse to the newest SHA. The quiet window therefore only needs to
coalesce near-simultaneous merges, not batch a whole feature — 60s is
sufficient. Builds still serialize (one Codemagic build at a time); this
only removes the artificial pre-tag delay.

## Changes
- `plan-desktop-release.py`: `AUTO_RELEASE_QUIET_SECONDS = 60` (was `10
* 60`).
- `check-release-process-guards.py`: guard updated to assert the new 60s
value.

## Verification
- `check-release-process-guards.py` passes; `pytest` on planner +
release-flow contract tests → **25 passed, 21 subtests**.
- Live: v0.12.113 is already building on Codemagic (manually tagged to
unblock the immediate case); this change makes future merges tag within
~60s automatically.

ci(desktop): build a beta candidate on every macOS merge to main (#10370)

## Summary

Trigger the desktop beta release pipeline on **every macOS-affecting
merge to main**, instead of only the hourly cron. New macOS code now
plans a candidate within minutes of the merge (past the planner's
10-minute quiet window) rather than waiting up to an hour.

## What changed
- `desktop_auto_release.yml`: added a `push` trigger scoped to `main` +
the desktop release paths (`desktop/macos/**`, the planner script, and
the two desktop workflows). Kept `workflow_dispatch` and the hourly
`schedule` as a backstop for merges that land inside the quiet window or
while a release is active.
- The push filter is intentionally coarse; `plan-desktop-release.py`
still does the precise releasable-change check (excluding
`CHANGELOG.json`/`AGENTS.md`/`changelog/`/`Backend-Rust/`), the
10-minute quiet window debounces bursts, and the one-active-release +
exact-SHA fences still admit at most one candidate at a time — so bursts
collapse to the newest SHA.

## Why it's safe (single candidate→beta authority preserved)
Every trigger runs the *same* fenced planner job. Beta promotion
(`desktop_promote_beta.yml`) keys off the **qualification's**
`workflow_dispatch` event, not this workflow's trigger, so adding `push`
here does not create a second promotion authority. Chained triggers
(`workflow_run`/`workflow_call`) that *could* form a second authority
remain forbidden by the contract test.

## Verification
- Updated the two contract tests that pinned the exact trigger shape
(`test_desktop_release_flow_contract.py`,
`test_plan_desktop_release.py`) to allow `push` while keeping the
"workflow_dispatch has no manual inputs" and "no chained-trigger second
authority" guards.
- `python3 -m pytest` on all three affected test files → **45 passed, 34
subtests**.
- `actionlint .github/workflows/desktop_auto_release.yml` → clean.
- `check-release-process-guards.py` → passed. YAML parses; triggers =
`[workflow_dispatch, push, schedule]`.

fix(web): prune deleted memories from selection on partial bulk-delete

When a bulk delete spans multiple 100-item chunks and a later chunk fails,
removeMemories already removed the earlier successful chunks from the list
but executeBulkDelete kept the ORIGINAL selectedIds (it only cleared on full
success). Retrying then re-sent ids the server had already deleted, which the
all-or-nothing endpoint rejects with 404 — blocking the remaining still-
selected memories.

removeMemories now returns { success, deletedIds } carrying the ids confirmed
deleted across successful chunks; executeBulkDelete prunes those from
selectedIds on partial failure so a retry only re-sends the failed / un-
attempted ids. The per-chunk optimistic UI update is unchanged.
